html:
Katarina Sunesdotter, also known as Katarina of Ymseborg [source needed], was born around 1215 and last mentioned in historical records on January 17, 1251. She died before February 1253. She was the Queen of Sweden, married to King Erik Eriksson (later nicknamed "the lame" and "the hunchbacked").

Katarina was the daughter of Sune Folkesson and Helena Sverkersdotter (daughter of King Sverker the Younger). In 1244, she married King Erik Eriksson, who was known as "the lame" and "the hunchbacked" (died February 2, 1250), but they had no children. On June 11, 1250, she made a significant donation to Gudhems Monastery, where she intended to retire. The same year, she bequeathed Söderköping to her sister Benedicta.

A sculpture depicting Katarina Sunesdotter is located at Hagatorget in Söderköping. The sculpture was created by S Dahlström in 1965. A wooden sculpture of her husband can be found in Hjo.

== Sources == Agneta Conradi Mattsson: Riseberga Monastery, Birger Brosa & the Filipssons, Scientific publications published by Örebro County Museum 2, 1998, ISBN 91-85642-24-X Dick Harrison: The Age of Jarls - A story about 13th-century Sweden, Ordfront, Stockholm, 2002, ISBN 91-7324-999-8

=== Notes ===

== External links == Wikimedia Commons has media related to Katarina Sunesdotter. http://sok.riksarkivet.se/SBL/Presentation.aspx?id=12402

How certain are we about this?

For people from the Middle Ages, there are often contemporary written sources (letters, chronicles, church documents), so the main facts are usually well-documented. However, these sources were mostly written by the Church and those in power, and they are unevenly preserved, which colors and limits our understanding of the past.
tldr: Katarina Sunesdotter was a Swedish queen in the 13th century who was married to King Eric Eriksson. She is known for having donated significant gifts to the church, and statues of her exist in both Söderköping and Hjo.
lattlast:
Katarina Sunesdotter lived during the Middle Ages and was queen of Sweden. She married King Erik Eriksson in 1244, but they did not have any children together. After the king's death, she donated a large amount of money and gifts to a monastery so that she could become a nun, meaning she wanted to live a life of prayer and silence within the church.

She was also important to the town of Söderköping, where she transferred ownership of the town to her sister Benedicta in 1250. Today, you can see a sculpture depicting her in Hagatorget square in Söderköping, and there is a wooden sculpture of her husband in the town of Hjo.

Because she lived so long ago, information about her is based on old texts from the church and those in power. These sources give us a picture of her life, but they may be limited because they were written by people with authority at that time.
